## Production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Production")]

### Development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development")]

> I think anybody ... who would have said that this would make a great main movie would have been laughed off. It's such an unlikely thing to do. So I think it's lovely to work on something and expose its potential to the point where a company like Marvel will say, 'Yes, this makes sense.'

—Dan Abnett, co-writer of the 2008 *Guardians of the Galaxy* relaunch, on the project[[94]](#cite_note-2012AugAbnettLanning-95)

[Marvel Studios](/wiki/Marvel_Studios "Marvel Studios") President [Kevin Feige](/wiki/Kevin_Feige "Kevin Feige") first mentioned *Guardians of the Galaxy* as a potential film at the 2010 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con"), stating, "There are some obscure titles, too, like *Guardians of the Galaxy*. I think they've been revamped recently in a fun way in the [comic] book."[[95]](#cite_note-2010SDCCFeige-96) Feige reiterated that sentiment in a September 2011 issue of *[Entertainment Weekly](/wiki/Entertainment_Weekly "Entertainment Weekly")*, saying, "There's an opportunity to do a big space epic, which *[Thor](/wiki/Thor_(film) "Thor (film)")* (2011) sort of hints at, in the cosmic side" of the [Marvel Cinematic Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe "Marvel Cinematic Universe") (MCU). Feige added, should the film be made, it would feature an ensemble of characters, similar to *[X-Men](/wiki/X-Men_(film) "X-Men (film)")* (2000) and *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* (2012).[[96]](#cite_note-2011SeptEW-97)

Feige announced that the film was in active development at the 2012 San Diego Comic-Con during the Marvel Studios panel, with an intended release date of August 1, 2014. He said the film's titular team would consist of the characters [Star-Lord](/wiki/Peter_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Peter Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Drax the Destroyer](/wiki/Drax_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Drax (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Gamora](/wiki/Gamora_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Gamora (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Groot](/wiki/Groot_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Groot (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and [Rocket](/wiki/Rocket_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Rocket (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[97]](#cite_note-2012SDCCPanel-98) Two pieces of concept art were also displayed, one of Rocket Raccoon, and one featuring the entire team.[[98]](#cite_note-2012SDCCArt-99) [Colin Trevorrow](/wiki/Colin_Trevorrow "Colin Trevorrow") had discussions to direct the film, but he turned it down as he was never a comic book fan.[[99]](#cite_note-100) In August 2012, [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn") entered talks to direct the film, beating out other contenders, including future MCU directors [Peyton Reed](/wiki/Peyton_Reed "Peyton Reed") and the duo [Anna Boden and Ryan Fleck](/wiki/Anna_Boden_and_Ryan_Fleck "Anna Boden and Ryan Fleck").[[100]](#cite_note-2012AugGunn-101) *The Avengers* director [Joss Whedon](/wiki/Joss_Whedon "Joss Whedon"), who signed a deal to creatively consult on all of the films leading up to [*The Avengers* sequel](/wiki/Avengers:_Age_of_Ultron "Avengers: Age of Ultron") (2015),[[101]](#cite_note-2012AugWhedon-102) was enthusiastic about the selection of Gunn to direct, saying "James [Gunn] is what makes me think it will work ... He is so off the wall, and so crazy, but so smart, such a craftsman and he builds from his heart. He loves the raccoon. Needs the raccoon ... He has a very twisted take on it, but it all comes from a real love for the material. It's going to be hard for [the human characters] to keep up".[[102]](#cite_note-2012SepWhedon-103)

#### Writing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Writing")]

[Nicole Perlman](/wiki/Nicole_Perlman "Nicole Perlman"), who was enrolled in Marvel's screenwriting program in 2009,[[103]](#cite_note-Screenplay-104) was offered several of their lesser known properties to base a screenplay on.[[104]](#cite_note-Perlman3-105) Out of those, Perlman chose [Dan Abnett](/wiki/Dan_Abnett "Dan Abnett") and [Andy Lanning](/wiki/Andy_Lanning "Andy Lanning")'s *[Guardians of the Galaxy](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(2008_team) "Guardians of the Galaxy (2008 team)")*, due to her interest in space and [science fiction](/wiki/Science_fiction "Science fiction"),[[104]](#cite_note-Perlman3-105) adding, "I think [Marvel] were a little taken aback when I chose *Guardians*, because there were ones that would make a lot more sense if you were a romantic-comedy writer or something like that".[[105]](#cite_note-Perlman-106) Perlman wrote 10 drafts between 2009 and 2011, in which she tried various combinations of characters for the team, including [the original members in the comics](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(1969_team) "Guardians of the Galaxy (1969 team)"), before settling on Abnett and Lanning's run since theirs "were the most fun".[[106]](#cite_note-PerlmanInverse-107) Early drafts centered on [Nova](/wiki/Nova_(Richard_Rider) "Nova (Richard Rider)"), before [Nate Moore](/wiki/Nate_Moore_(film_producer) "Nate Moore (film producer)"), the head of the studios' writing program, suggested it change to Star-Lord.[[107]](#cite_note-MCUReignOfMarvelStudios-108) Subsequent drafts saw Star-Lord reimagined to be closer to a [Han Solo](/wiki/Han_Solo "Han Solo")-type character; the inclusion of 1980s and 1990s music from Perlman's childhood, which would become the 1970s and 1980s pop mixtape in Gunn's later drafts; and the eventual opening scene of the film on a desolate planet that harkened back to a memory Perlman had at [Disneyland](/wiki/Disneyland "Disneyland") viewing a drained-out [Submarine Voyage](/wiki/Submarine_Voyage "Submarine Voyage").[[106]](#cite_note-PerlmanInverse-107) Gunn was brought in in early 2012 to contribute to the script,[[105]](#cite_note-Perlman-106) eventually rewriting the script entirely because "it didn't work" for him; he would use the film *[The Dirty Dozen](/wiki/The_Dirty_Dozen "The Dirty Dozen")* (1967) as a reference to convey his ideas of the film to Marvel.[[108]](#cite_note-2014JulGunn-109)

Gunn later explained that Perlman's draft was very different from the script he used during filming, including a different story, [character arcs](/wiki/Character_arc "Character arc") and no [Walkman](/wiki/Walkman "Walkman"); he stated, "In Nicole's script everything is pretty different ... it's not about the same stuff. But that's how the [WGA](/wiki/Writers_Guild_of_America,_West "Writers Guild of America, West") works."[[109]](#cite_note-GunnFilmDivider-110) The 2012 screenplay also had [J'son](/wiki/J%27son_(character) "J'son (character)") as an emperor and [Peter Quill / Star-Lord](/wiki/Peter_Quill_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Peter Quill (Marvel Cinematic Universe)")'s father just like in the comic books. When Marvel asked Joss Whedon to revise the script ahead of *Age of Ultron*, Whedon particularly detested this reveal and suggested Marvel to not make Peter part of royalty;[[110]](#cite_note-PetersOriginalFather-111) Whedon suggested Gunn to make the story "weirder" after reading the early draft.[[111]](#cite_note-MakeItWeirder-112) Throughout the writing process, there was some debate on whether keeping Rocket as a member of the Guardians or even featuring the character at all in the film due to concerns that he could come off as "too cartoonish", hence why a few drafts excluded the character, but Perlman felt that the character's inclusion was necessary and was happy at Feige letting her keep him in the script due to him being a great Rocket fan.[[112]](#cite_note-RocketsAlmostAbsence-113) In August 2012, Marvel Studios hired writer Chris McCoy to rewrite Perlman's script,[[113]](#cite_note-2012AugMcCoy-114) but was not credited in the final film.[[61]](#cite_note-2013JulyMarvel-62)

Gunn revealed that character introductions were the "hardest scenes to crack", with [Thanos](/wiki/Thanos_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Thanos (Marvel Cinematic Universe)")' introduction being the most difficult. He felt that "having Thanos be in that scene was more helpful to the [MCU] than it was to *Guardians of the Galaxy*," yet he still wanted Thanos in the film, without "[belittling] the actual antagonist of the film, which is Ronan". To solve his dilemma, Gunn chose to have Ronan kill "The Other", Thanos' [vizier](/wiki/Vizier "Vizier"), saying, "I thought that was interesting, because we've had the Other, who's obviously very powerful even in comparison to [Loki](/wiki/Loki_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Loki (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and then we see Ronan wipe his ass with him. So that I liked, but even that was sort of difficult, because it played as funnier when I first wrote it, and the humor didn't work so much".[[114]](#cite_note-GunnVultureThanos-115) Perlman's original script had Thanos as the main villain of the film, but Whedon asked Gunn if he could not have Thanos as the main antagonist and Gunn agreed, reducing Thanos' role in the plot.[[115]](#cite_note-PerlmansThanosScript-116)

### Pre-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Pre-production")]

In September 2012, Gunn confirmed that he had signed on to direct the film and rewrite the script.[[116]](#cite_note-2012SepGunn-117) By the end of November, [Joel Edgerton](/wiki/Joel_Edgerton "Joel Edgerton"), [Jack Huston](/wiki/Jack_Huston "Jack Huston"), [Jim Sturgess](/wiki/Jim_Sturgess "Jim Sturgess"), and [Eddie Redmayne](/wiki/Eddie_Redmayne "Eddie Redmayne") signed deals to test for the role of Peter Quill, as did [Lee Pace](/wiki/Lee_Pace "Lee Pace"),[[117]](#cite_note-2012NovTestDeals-118) which he confirmed a week later in early December.[[50]](#cite_note-2012DecPace-51) Other actors who were considered for the role included *[Thor: The Dark World](/wiki/Thor:_The_Dark_World "Thor: The Dark World")* (2013) actor [Zachary Levi](/wiki/Zachary_Levi "Zachary Levi"),[[118]](#cite_note-2012Dec21Quill-119) [Adam Brody](/wiki/Adam_Brody "Adam Brody"),[[119]](#cite_note-2023AugBrody-120) [Joseph Gordon-Levitt](/wiki/Joseph_Gordon-Levitt "Joseph Gordon-Levitt"),[[120]](#cite_note-2013JanJGL-121) [Michael Rosenbaum](/wiki/Michael_Rosenbaum "Michael Rosenbaum"),[[121]](#cite_note-2013JanRosenbaum-122) and [John Gallagher Jr.](/wiki/John_Gallagher_Jr. "John Gallagher Jr.")[[122]](#cite_note-GallagherStarLord-123) [Chris Pratt](/wiki/Chris_Pratt "Chris Pratt") was cast in the role in February 2013, as part of a multi-film deal that he signed with Marvel.[[15]](#cite_note-HuffingtonPostNov2013-16)[[123]](#cite_note-2013FebPratt-124) [Glenn Howerton](/wiki/Glenn_Howerton "Glenn Howerton") was Gunn's second choice for the role.[[124]](#cite_note-Howerton-125)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/James_Gunn_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-James_Gunn_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Gunn_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

Gunn promoting the film at the 2013 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con")

In January 2013, filming was scheduled to take place at [Shepperton Studios](/wiki/Shepperton_Studios "Shepperton Studios") in London, United Kingdom,[[125]](#cite_note-2013JanShepperton-126) and Marvel Studios announced that the film would be released in [3D](/wiki/3D_film "3D film").[[126]](#cite_note-2013Jan3D-127) [Victoria Alonso](/wiki/Victoria_Alonso "Victoria Alonso"), an executive producer on the film, said that filming would begin in June. She also said that both Rocket Raccoon and Groot would be created through a combination of CGI and motion capture, going on to say that "You can't do any motion capture with a raccoon—they won't let you put the suit on. But we will do [rotomation](/wiki/Rotoscoping "Rotoscoping"), probably, for some of the behavior ... we definitely will have performers to emulate what James Gunn will lead to be, the behavior and the performance. He's very clear on where he wants to take the characters."[[127]](#cite_note-2013FebAlonso-128) In March 2013, Feige discussed *Guardians of the Galaxy* in relation to the Marvel Cinematic Universe, saying, "It's much more of a [standalone](/wiki/Sequel#Standalone_sequel "Sequel") film. It takes place in the same universe. And when we've been on the other side of that universe in other movies, you might see those characteristics in Guardians, but the Avengers are not involved with what's happening out there at this time." Feige also stated that 95% of the film would take place in space.[[128]](#cite_note-2013MarchSFX-129) In mid-March, [Dave Bautista](/wiki/Dave_Bautista "Dave Bautista") was signed to play [Drax the Destroyer](/wiki/Drax_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Drax (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[129]](#cite_note-2013MarchBautista-130) Other actors who had been considered for the role included [Isaiah Mustafa](/wiki/Isaiah_Mustafa "Isaiah Mustafa"), [Brian Patrick Wade](/wiki/Brian_Patrick_Wade "Brian Patrick Wade"), [Jason Momoa](/wiki/Jason_Momoa "Jason Momoa"),[[129]](#cite_note-2013MarchBautista-130) and [Chadwick Boseman](/wiki/Chadwick_Boseman "Chadwick Boseman").[[130]](#cite_note-MCUReignVanityFairExcerpt-131) Momoa chose not to continue after auditioning, feeling the character was too similar to other parts he had played in the past, and he did not want to further add to his typecasting as the "brute" character.[[131]](#cite_note-MomoaDecline-132) By the following week, sculptor [Brian Muir](/wiki/Brian_Muir_(sculptor) "Brian Muir (sculptor)"), who sculpted [Darth Vader](/wiki/Darth_Vader "Darth Vader")'s mask for *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)")* (1977), was revealed to be working on the film.[[132]](#cite_note-2013MarchMuir-133)

In early April 2013, [Zoe Saldaña](/wiki/Zoe_Salda%C3%B1a "Zoe Saldaña") entered into negotiations to star as [Gamora](/wiki/Gamora_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Gamora (Marvel Cinematic Universe)") in the film, and it was confirmed she had been cast later that month.[[71]](#cite_note-2013AprilLovibond-72)[[133]](#cite_note-2013AprilSaldaña-134) [Amanda Seyfried](/wiki/Amanda_Seyfried "Amanda Seyfried") had been offered the role, but declined due to the excessive hours of make-up required for the role, and her uncertainty over the film's commercial viability.[[134]](#cite_note-SeyfriedGamora-135) [Lupita Nyong'o](/wiki/Lupita_Nyong%27o "Lupita Nyong'o") also auditioned for the role.[[130]](#cite_note-MCUReignVanityFairExcerpt-131) Also in April, [Michael Rooker](/wiki/Michael_Rooker "Michael Rooker") joined the film's cast as [Yondu Udonta](/wiki/Yondu_Udonta_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Yondu Udonta (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"),[[52]](#cite_note-2013AprilRooker-53) and it was announced that [Ophelia Lovibond](/wiki/Ophelia_Lovibond "Ophelia Lovibond") had been cast in a supporting role.[[71]](#cite_note-2013AprilLovibond-72) By this point in time, [Lee Pace](/wiki/Lee_Pace "Lee Pace") was in final negotiations to play the villain of the film.[[135]](#cite_note-2013AprilPace-136) In May, Marvel offered [John C. Reilly](/wiki/John_C._Reilly "John C. Reilly") the role of [Rhomann Dey](/wiki/Rhomann_Dey "Rhomann Dey").[[136]](#cite_note-2013MayHitFix-137) At the same time, it was disclosed that filmmakers were looking at actors including [Hugh Laurie](/wiki/Hugh_Laurie "Hugh Laurie"), [Alan Rickman](/wiki/Alan_Rickman "Alan Rickman"), and [Ken Watanabe](/wiki/Ken_Watanabe "Ken Watanabe"), for another role, and that [Christopher Markus and Stephen McFeely](/wiki/Christopher_Markus_and_Stephen_McFeely "Christopher Markus and Stephen McFeely") were providing finishing touches to the script.[[136]](#cite_note-2013MayHitFix-137) A few days later, [Glenn Close](/wiki/Glenn_Close "Glenn Close") was cast as the head of the [Nova Corps](/wiki/Nova_Corps "Nova Corps") in the film,[[62]](#cite_note-2013MayClose-63) followed shortly by the casting of [Karen Gillan](/wiki/Karen_Gillan "Karen Gillan") as the film's lead female villain.[[137]](#cite_note-2013MayGillan-138) By June 2013, [Benicio del Toro](/wiki/Benicio_del_Toro "Benicio del Toro") was cast in the film, as part of a multi-film deal with Marvel Studios.[[65]](#cite_note-2013MayDelToro-66) Later in the month, it was confirmed that Reilly had been cast as Rhomann Dey.[[60]](#cite_note-2013JuneReilly-61)

Special effects makeup designer David White took head and body casts of actors, such as Bautista, to experiment with materials used to create the characters. White said, "James always pushed for practical and makeup effects. He wanted, like me, to see the real deal there on set." White was careful not to use "modern" creature designs to ensure they did not fall short in Gunn's uniquely envisioned world. White and his team created upwards of 1,000 prosthetic makeup applications and 2,000 molds of different-colored aliens.[[138]](#cite_note-WhiteMakeup-139) For the specific aesthetic look to the film, Gunn wanted to create "a colorful science-fiction world", and include elements of 1950s and '60s pulp movies, citing the Ravagers' spaceships, which he compared to muscle cars, as an example of the latter.[[139]](#cite_note-GunnDesign-140) Science fiction artist [Chris Foss](/wiki/Chris_Foss "Chris Foss") inspired and helped design the final look of some of the spacecrafts that appear in the film.[[140]](#cite_note-ChrisFoss-141) The *[Mass Effect](/wiki/Mass_Effect "Mass Effect")* video game series, [Flash Gordon](/wiki/Flash_Gordon "Flash Gordon"), *[Farscape](/wiki/Farscape "Farscape")* and *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* were primary inspirations for Gunn on the film.[[141]](#cite_note-GunnInspirations-142)

### Filming

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Filming")]

Principal photography began around July 6, 2013, in [London](/wiki/London "London"), United Kingdom,[[58]](#cite_note-2013JulyComicCon-59) under the working title of *Full Tilt*.[[8]](#cite_note-SuperheroHypePg1-9) Filming took place at Shepperton Studios and [Longcross Studios](/wiki/Longcross_Studios "Longcross Studios").[[125]](#cite_note-2013JanShepperton-126)[[142]](#cite_note-DavisInterview-143) Later in July, Gunn and the film's cast flew from London to attend San Diego Comic-Con, where it was revealed that Pace would play Ronan the Accuser, Gillan would be [Nebula](/wiki/Nebula_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Nebula (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), del Toro as [the Collector](/wiki/Taneleer_Tivan_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Taneleer Tivan (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), and that [Djimon Hounsou](/wiki/Djimon_Hounsou "Djimon Hounsou") had been cast as [Korath](/wiki/Korath_the_Pursuer_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Korath the Pursuer (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[58]](#cite_note-2013JulyComicCon-59)[[61]](#cite_note-2013JulyMarvel-62) Close was later revealed to play [Nova Prime Irani Rael](/wiki/Irani_Rael_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Irani Rael (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[9]](#cite_note-StitchKingdom-10)[[61]](#cite_note-2013JulyMarvel-62) Also at San Diego Comic-Con, Feige stated that Thanos would be a part of the film as the "mastermind".[[143]](#cite_note-2013JulyFeige-144) On August 11, 2013, filming began at London's [Millennium Bridge](/wiki/Millennium_Bridge,_London "Millennium Bridge, London"),[[144]](#cite_note-Entertainmentwise-145) which was selected as a double for [Xandar](/wiki/Xandar_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Xandar (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[24]](#cite_note-CBRSetVisit-25) In August 2013, Marvel announced that [Bradley Cooper](/wiki/Bradley_Cooper "Bradley Cooper") would voice Rocket.[[38]](#cite_note-CooperOfficial-39) On September 3, 2013, Gunn said that filming was "a little over half[way]" complete.[[145]](#cite_note-2013SeptMTV-146) Also in September, [Vin Diesel](/wiki/Vin_Diesel "Vin Diesel") stated that he was voicing Groot.[[28]](#cite_note-DieselConfirm-29) However, Marvel did not confirm Diesel's involvement in the film at the time.[[43]](#cite_note-RocketStandIn-44)[[44]](#cite_note-2013NovDelToro-45)[[146]](#cite_note-HuffingtonPostOct2013-147) On October 12, 2013, Gunn announced on social media that filming had completed.[[147]](#cite_note-FilmingEnding-148)

[Director of photography](/wiki/Director_of_photography "Director of photography") [Ben Davis](/wiki/Ben_Davis_(cinematographer) "Ben Davis (cinematographer)") used [Arri Alexa XT](/wiki/Arri_Alexa#Alexa_XT "Arri Alexa") cameras for the film, saying, "I'm traditionally a photochemical fan, but going with the digital format was the right way for this movie ... the Alexa [provided] the right look for this particular film". During the opening scenes in the 1980s, Davis chose JDC Cooke Xtal (Crystal) Express anamorphic prime lenses because they "had more anamorphic artifacts and aberrations, which [he] felt added something". Davis used spherical [Panavision](/wiki/Panavision "Panavision") Primos for the rest of the film. Additionally, Davis worked closely with production designer Charles Wood to achieve the correct lighting looks for each scene.[[142]](#cite_note-DavisInterview-143) Dealing with two fully CGI characters forced Davis to shoot scenes multiple times, usually once with the references for the characters and once without them in the shot.[[148]](#cite_note-DavisInterview2-149)

Gunn revealed that his brother, [Sean Gunn](/wiki/Sean_Gunn "Sean Gunn"), took on multiple roles during the filming process, such as standing in for Rocket, which he noted was beneficial for the other actors, including Saldaña, Pratt, and Bautista, who responded positively to Sean and his on-set performances.[[30]](#cite_note-SeanGunnThanos-31) Sean was first hired to play Kraglin Obfonteri, but James hired him as a stand-in on set for Rocket because he knew he could trust in him until they could figure out how to portray Rocket, though they knew Sean would not voice the character, with Cooper being cast to voice Rocket very late in development.[[149]](#cite_note-SeanGunnDiscussingFilm-150) Special effects makeup designer David White made two life-size versions of Rocket and a bust of Groot as aids for visual effects, with White saying, "it gives [the filmmakers] a good indication of where visual effects needs to pick up and whether Rocket can actually reach certain things or use certain devices".[[31]](#cite_note-RocketGrootBI-32) These busts were also used to see how the on-set lighting would affect the characters, to assist with the visual effects lighting process.[[31]](#cite_note-RocketGrootBI-32)

In January 2015, Disney revealed that the film came in "slightly over the agreed budget" at $232.3 million, with Disney receiving a rebate of $36.4 million from the British government.[[2]](#cite_note-ActualBudget-2) It was previously estimated to have had a $170 million budget.[[150]](#cite_note-Tracking-151)

### Post-production

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Post-production")]

In November 2013, Gunn stated that he attempted to use as many practical effects as possible while filming to aid the use of CGI and motion capture during post-production, saying, "Our sets are enormous. We have a prison that is 350,000 pounds of steel. Anybody who knows me knows I love the mix of practical and CGI effects ... I can't wait for people to see it, because it's astonishingly beautiful."[[151]](#cite_note-GunnEffects-152) After the release of *Thor: The Dark World*, Feige stated that the [Infinity Stones](/wiki/Infinity_Stones "Infinity Stones") would be a focus in the film, as well as going forward into the Phase Three slate of films within the MCU.[[152]](#cite_note-InfinityStones-153) Gunn originally wrote the scene of the Collector explaining about the origins of the Infinity Stones in one hour and a half, only to then be told by Marvel Studios that they were incidentally thinking about including the Power Stone in his film, an idea that Gunn found cool to use, thus subsequent MCU filmmakers involved in the [long-planned story](/wiki/Production_of_Avengers:_Infinity_War_and_Avengers:_Endgame#Background "Production of Avengers: Infinity War and Avengers: Endgame") of *[Avengers: Infinity War](/wiki/Avengers:_Infinity_War "Avengers: Infinity War")* (2018) based themselves on Gunn's backstory for the Stones.[[153]](#cite_note-GunnCameUpInfinityStonesBackstory-154) In a separate interview for *The Dark World* in November, Feige added that a third, unknown Infinity Stone would be seen in the film,[[154]](#cite_note-SlashFilmNov2013-155) referred to as the "Power Stone" by the Collector.[[155]](#cite_note-VultureThanos-156)[[156]](#cite_note-IBPowerStone-157) The [mid-credits scene](/wiki/Mid-credits_scene "Mid-credits scene") in *The Dark World* revealed Lovibond's role as the Collector's slave, later named Carina.[[72]](#cite_note-2014AprilLovibond-73) In December 2013, Marvel confirmed that Diesel would voice Groot.[[157]](#cite_note-DieselMarvelOfficial-158)

A few weeks of additional filming, involving the film's main cast and crew, occurred in March 2014 at [Walt Disney Studios](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_(Burbank) "Walt Disney Studios (Burbank)") in [Burbank, California](/wiki/Burbank,_California "Burbank, California").[[158]](#cite_note-GunnAddnlFilming-159)[[159]](#cite_note-GunnAddnlFilming2-160)[[160]](#cite_note-SaldañaAddnlFilming-161) In April 2014, Gunn described Thanos as the "head of the snake" in the film, and confirmed he would appear via performance capture.[[11]](#cite_note-SlashFilmApr2014-12) In May 2014, Gunn stated that the film features an "enormous" amount of smaller and minor characters from the Marvel Universe, adding that he felt the film had the most characters overall of any Marvel Studios film to date.[[161]](#cite_note-IGNMay2014-162) Costume supervisor Dan Grace added to this by saying, "We really, really get the feeling of the scale and scope of the galaxy. We visit five planets, we see a hundred different races." The film introduces the alien race [Sakaaran](/wiki/Planet_Hulk "Planet Hulk"), who act as Ronan's mercenaries, as a replacement to the [Badoon](/wiki/Badoon "Badoon"), as the Badoon film rights belonged to [20th Century Fox](/wiki/20th_Century_Fox "20th Century Fox").[[47]](#cite_note-SuperheroHypePg3-48)

By the end of May, [Josh Brolin](/wiki/Josh_Brolin "Josh Brolin") was revealed as the voice of Thanos,[[67]](#cite_note-ThanosCast-68)[[68]](#cite_note-BrolinThanos-69) with Feige confirming in July that Brolin also provided the performance capture for the character.[[69]](#cite_note-BrolinPerformance-70) Whedon assisted Gunn as well in casting Brolin.[[162]](#cite_note-2014JulyFeige-163) Brolin was filming *[Everest](/wiki/Everest_(2015_film) "Everest (2015 film)")* (2015) when Marvel Studios approached him to play Thanos with information about the character, explaining to him that his role in *Guardians* would consist of a [cameo appearance](/wiki/Cameo_appearance "Cameo appearance"), with larger roles planned for later installments.[[163]](#cite_note-BrolinsContract-164) Feige explained that Brolin was "totally intrigued" when approached for the role, having been a fan of Marvel Studios' past films.[[69]](#cite_note-BrolinPerformance-70) Thanos communicates via hologram for much of the film, though he does appear in a scene with Ronan and Nebula.[[155]](#cite_note-VultureThanos-156) In June 2014, Feige added that Thanos and his followers are "the biggest piece of connective tissue that will eventually lead us back into *Avengers* films in the future".[[164]](#cite_note-FeigeTotalFilm-165)

On July 7, 2014, Gunn announced on social media that he had completed work on the film.[[165]](#cite_note-GotGComplete-166) In August, regarding the post-credit scene, Gunn revealed that the scene did not involve [Howard the Duck](/wiki/Howard_the_Duck "Howard the Duck") when it was originally filmed, rather he was added during post-production,[[166]](#cite_note-GunnHowardDuck-167) a decision made by "some combination of [Gunn] and the editor Fred Raskin".[[80]](#cite_note-Corlett-81) As the decision to add the character was made late in the post-production process, he had to be designed that day, before being handed off to Sony Pictures Imageworks to animate. Also in August, regarding the pre-credit scene of Baby Groot dancing, Gunn stated that he himself danced to provide motion reference for the animators, and that the decision was made to place the scene before the credits, rather than during or after them, because of positive responses from a test audience, which made Marvel and Gunn feel that they did not want "people walking out and missing this thing".[[80]](#cite_note-Corlett-81) Another late change halfway in production came when Marvel decided that the Reality Stone would be red in *The Dark World*. Because the Power Stone was originally meant to be red as well, Gunn was asked to change its color from red to purple, which he accepted as, by his own admission, liked that color more.[[167]](#cite_note-GunnRedPowerStone-168) Marvel used design firm Sarofsky once again for the film's title sequences, after liking their work for *[Captain America: The Winter Soldier](/wiki/Captain_America:_The_Winter_Soldier "Captain America: The Winter Soldier")* (2014). Sarofsky developed a custom [typeface](/wiki/Typeface "Typeface") based on the font used in the teaser posters for the opening credits, which was tinted orange to offer a better contrast to the film's blue and gray imagery. One of the typography solutions offered before the final product wound up being repurposed as the locator cards seen throughout the film.[[168]](#cite_note-Sarofsky-169)

#### Visual effects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Visual effects")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/Gardens_by_the_Bay_South_viewed_from_Sands_Sky_Park%2C_Marina_Bay_Sands_Hotel%2C_Singapore.jpg/250px-Gardens_by_the_Bay_South_viewed_from_Sands_Sky_Park%2C_Marina_Bay_Sands_Hotel%2C_Singapore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gardens_by_the_Bay_South_viewed_from_Sands_Sky_Park,_Marina_Bay_Sands_Hotel,_Singapore.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/bf/Xandar_in_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_%28film%29.jpg/250px-Xandar_in_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_%28film%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Xandar_in_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film).jpg)

The [Gardens by the Bay](/wiki/Gardens_by_the_Bay "Gardens by the Bay") (top) in [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore "Singapore") helped inspire the look of the planet Xandar (bottom).[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170)

The film featured 2,750 visual effects shots,[[170]](#cite_note-VFXShots-171) which make up approximately 90% of the film.[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) The visual effects were created by: [Moving Picture Company](/wiki/Moving_Picture_Company "Moving Picture Company") (MPC), who worked on creating Groot, as well as [Morag](/wiki/Morag_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Morag (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Xandar](/wiki/Xandar_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Xandar (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), the *[Dark Aster](/wiki/Dark_Aster_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Dark Aster (Marvel Cinematic Universe)")* and the final battle on Xandar;[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) [Framestore](/wiki/Framestore "Framestore"), who worked on creating Rocket, extending the Kyln prison set and constructing Knowhere;[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170)[[171]](#cite_note-FramestoreRocket-172) [Luma Pictures](/wiki/Luma_Pictures "Luma Pictures"), who worked on Thanos;[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) [Method Studios](/wiki/Method_Studios "Method Studios"), who worked on creating the Orb opening and revealing its powers, as well as the holographic displays at the Nova Corps command center;[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) Lola VFX; [Cantina Creative](/wiki/Cantina_Creative "Cantina Creative"); [Sony Pictures Imageworks](/wiki/Sony_Pictures_Imageworks "Sony Pictures Imageworks"), who worked on Howard the Duck and creating the *Dark Aster* shots with MPC;[[80]](#cite_note-Corlett-81)[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) CoSA VFX; Secret Lab; Rise Visual Effects Studios; and Technicolor VFX.[[172]](#cite_note-VarietyReview-173)[[173]](#cite_note-MethodVFX-174) Pre- and post-visualizations were done by Proof and The Third Floor, with Proof also contributing to the creation of Rocket and Groot.[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170)

Producer Nik Korda noted how helpful it was to have Sean Gunn and Krystian Godlewski portray Rocket and Groot on set, as it provided references for lighting and on-set performances to the animators.[[31]](#cite_note-RocketGrootBI-32) When creating Groot, MPC realized early on that his eyes would be essential in maintaining the character's human qualities, as his face couldn't move in the way that humans' do. MPC visual effects supervisor Nicolas Aithadi explained that, "When you look at humans what makes the eyes interesting is the imperfections—trying to make these two irises not aimed at the same place—trying to make them strange and look more human."[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) Textures for Groot came from a number of sources, including inspiration from a botanical garden in London, and the character was modeled as individual branches, rigged individually, to simulate a muscle system for the character.[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170)

One of the major challenges for Framestore in creating Rocket was his fur. Framestore's Rachel Williams explained that, since "raccoon fur is made up from a layer of short fine hair and a layer of longer thicker hairs", these layers were separated and animated individually, removing the need to use "guide hairs" to control the movements of thick sections of fur.[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170) Framestore and MPC worked closely sharing assets, to ensure shots of Rocket at MPC would match the Rocket created by Framestore, and vice versa for when Groot was needed by the other studio.[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170)

To give Thanos "the performance and the weight that he deserved", Luma Pictures created a new facial animation system to re-create Josh Brolin as a fully CG character, using his "eyes, some of his cheek, how his muscles move when he talks". The character's large jaw, and the deep grooves that run down his face, had "to be carefully planned out with the movement of his face".[[169]](#cite_note-FXGuide-170)

## Music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Music")]

Main article: [*Guardians of the Galaxy* (soundtrack)](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(soundtrack) "Guardians of the Galaxy (soundtrack)")

In August 2013, Gunn revealed that [Tyler Bates](/wiki/Tyler_Bates "Tyler Bates") would be composing the film's score. Gunn stated that Bates would write some of the score first so that he can film to the music, as opposed to scoring to the film.[[174]](#cite_note-Bates-175) In February 2014, Gunn revealed that the film would incorporate songs from the 1960s and 1970s, such as "[Hooked on a Feeling](/wiki/Hooked_on_a_Feeling_(song) "Hooked on a Feeling (song)")", on a mixtape in Quill's [Walkman](/wiki/Walkman "Walkman"), which acts as a way for him to stay connected to the Earth, home, and family he lost.[[175]](#cite_note-TotalFilmTrailer6-176) In May 2014, Gunn added that using the songs from the '60s and '70s were "cultural reference points", saying, "It's striking the balance throughout the whole movie, through something that is very unique, but also something that is easily accessible to people at the same time. The music and the Earth stuff is one of those touchstones that we have to remind us that, yeah, [Quill] is a real person from planet Earth who's just like you and me. Except that he's in this big outer space adventure."[[161]](#cite_note-IGNMay2014-162)

When choosing the songs, Gunn revealed he "started the process by reading the *[Billboard](/wiki/Billboard_(magazine) "Billboard (magazine)")* charts for all of the top hits of the '70s", downloading "a few hundred" songs that were "semi-familiar—ones you recognize but might not be able to name off the top of your head" and creating a playlist for all the songs that would fit the film tonally. He added that he "would listen to the playlist on my speakers around the house—sometimes I would be inspired to create a scene around a song, and other times I had a scene that needed music and I would listen through the playlist, visualizing various songs, figuring out which would work the best."[[176]](#cite_note-VultureMusic-177) Most of the songs were played on set to help "the actors and the camera operators find the perfect groove for the shot", with [David Bowie](/wiki/David_Bowie "David Bowie")'s "[Moonage Daydream](/wiki/Moonage_Daydream "Moonage Daydream")" the only song chosen and added during post-production. Gunn also said that the opening scenes were designed with "Hooked on a Feeling" in mind; however, once Gunn discovered "[Come and Get Your Love](/wiki/Come_and_Get_Your_Love "Come and Get Your Love")", the song used in the sequence, Gunn felt it was a "better fit."[[176]](#cite_note-VultureMusic-177)

Three albums were released by [Hollywood Records](/wiki/Hollywood_Records "Hollywood Records") on July 29, 2014: The film's score, *Guardians of the Galaxy (Original Score)*, which features the music composed by Bates for the film;[[177]](#cite_note-Score-178) *Guardians of the Galaxy: Awesome Mix Vol. 1 (Original Motion Picture Soundtrack)*, which comprises the twelve songs from Quill's mixtape; and a deluxe edition featuring both albums.[[178]](#cite_note-Soundtracks-179) By August 2014, the album which mirrored Quill's mixtape had reached the top of the [*Billboard* 200](/wiki/Billboard_200 "Billboard 200") chart, becoming the first soundtrack album in history consisting entirely of previously released songs to top the chart.[[179]](#cite_note-AwesomeMixChart-180) Hollywood Records also released a cassette version of the *Awesome Mix Vol. 1* soundtrack on November 28, 2014, as an exclusive to [Record Store Day](/wiki/Record_Store_Day "Record Store Day") participants. The cassette, which is the first cassette [Disney Music Group](/wiki/Disney_Music_Group "Disney Music Group") has released since 2003, comes with a digital download version of the album.[[180]](#cite_note-AwesomeMixCassette-181)

## Marketing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Marketing")]

### Promotion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Promotion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_cast_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg/250px-Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_cast_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)](/wiki/File:Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_cast_by_Gage_Skidmore.jpg)

The cast and crew of *Guardians of the Galaxy* at the 2013 [San Diego Comic-Con](/wiki/San_Diego_Comic-Con "San Diego Comic-Con") (L-R: producer [Kevin Feige](/wiki/Kevin_Feige "Kevin Feige"), Bautista, director [James Gunn](/wiki/James_Gunn "James Gunn"), del Toro, Saldaña, Hounsou, Pace, Rooker, Gillan, and Pratt)

At Disney's [D23](/wiki/Disney_D23 "Disney D23") Expo in August 2013, Feige presented footage first revealed at San Diego Comic-Con in July 2013.[[181]](#cite_note-D23-182) The first trailer for the film debuted on *[Jimmy Kimmel Live!](/wiki/Jimmy_Kimmel_Live! "Jimmy Kimmel Live!")* on February 18, 2014, with a special introduction by Chris Pratt.[[10]](#cite_note-USATodayFeb2014-11) The *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* said the trailer delivered "some spirited alien action, with exploding spaceships and muscled bad guys, not to mention a few purely comedic moments," while spotlighting "a number of the offbeat characters."[[182]](#cite_note-LATimesTrailer-183) *[Total Film](/wiki/Total_Film "Total Film")* noted the similarity of the trailer's opening clip to the 1981 film, *[Raiders of the Lost Ark](/wiki/Raiders_of_the_Lost_Ark "Raiders of the Lost Ark")*, which Gunn stated was a big influence for *Guardians*,[[183]](#cite_note-TotalFilmTrailer1-184) and made note of the "edgy" humor used throughout.[[184]](#cite_note-TotalFilmTrailer5-185) *Total Film* also noted the similarity of the trailer to the one debuted at Comic-Con and D23, with a few new shots featuring Nebula and the Collector.[[185]](#cite_note-TotalFilmTrailer9-186)[[186]](#cite_note-TotalFilmTrailer10-187) Social media response to the trailer was strong, with 88,000 mentions across [Twitter](/wiki/Twitter "Twitter"), [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook") and various blogs in a 12-hour period. Those numbers were comparable to trailers for other superhero films like *[Man of Steel](/wiki/Man_of_Steel_(film) "Man of Steel (film)")* and *[The Amazing Spider-Man 2](/wiki/The_Amazing_Spider-Man_2 "The Amazing Spider-Man 2")*, and noteworthy for the late night-time period in which it premiered.[[187]](#cite_note-TheWrapTrailer-188) The trailer received 22.8 million views in the 24 hours after it debuted.[[188]](#cite_note-TrailerViews-189) After the debut of the trailer, [Blue Swede](/wiki/Blue_Swede "Blue Swede")'s version of "[Hooked on a Feeling](/wiki/Hooked_on_a_Feeling_(song) "Hooked on a Feeling (song)")", which was prominently used throughout the trailer, went up 700% in sales the following day.[[189]](#cite_note-HookedOnAFeelingSales-190) The line from Serafinowicz's character, "What a bunch of a-holes", was only intended to be featured in the trailers, but due to its positive reception, was included in the final cut of the film.[[80]](#cite_note-Corlett-81) Gunn noted that a different teaser was almost released "that was very cheesy [and] sold the movie as something different than what it was", as it tested better than the version that was ultimately released. Gunn credited the marketing teams at Marvel and Disney for having faith in the latter version, despite how it tested, over choosing the version that would have mis-marketed the film.[[190]](#cite_note-CheesyTrailer-191)

In March 2014, the *[Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D.](/wiki/Agents_of_S.H.I.E.L.D. "Agents of S.H.I.E.L.D.")* episode "[T.A.H.I.T.I.](/wiki/T.A.H.I.T.I. "T.A.H.I.T.I.")" introduced the Kree race to the MCU, which began a storyline that recurs throughout the series and involves finding a hidden Kree city.[[191]](#cite_note-BennetKree-192)[[192]](#cite_note-BennetBellKree-193) Also in March, [ABC](/wiki/American_Broadcasting_Company "American Broadcasting Company") aired a one-hour [television special](/wiki/Television_special "Television special") titled, *[Marvel Studios: Assembling a Universe](/wiki/Marvel_Studios:_Assembling_a_Universe "Marvel Studios: Assembling a Universe")*, which included a sneak peek of *Guardians of the Galaxy*.[[193]](#cite_note-TVSpecial-194) Starting in April 2014, a two-part comic was released, titled *[Marvel's Guardians of the Galaxy Prelude](/wiki/Marvel%27s_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Prelude "Marvel's Guardians of the Galaxy Prelude")*. Written by [Dan Abnett](/wiki/Dan_Abnett "Dan Abnett") and [Andy Lanning](/wiki/Andy_Lanning "Andy Lanning"), with art by Wellington Alves, the first issue focused on the background of Nebula and her bond with Thanos; the second issue featured the adventures of Rocket and Groot before they join forces with the other Guardians.[[194]](#cite_note-PreludeComic-195) In June 2014, Mike Pasciullo, senior VP of Marvel Studios, described the marketing approach for *Guardians of the Galaxy* by saying that Marvel had "built a robust marketing program for the theatrical release that carefully integrates traditional advertising, social media, digital marketing, strategic promotional partnerships, organic entertainment integrations [and] publicity."[[195]](#cite_note-VarietyMarketingJun2014-196)

In July 2014, Marvel launched a [viral marketing](/wiki/Viral_marketing "Viral marketing") campaign for the film called "Galaxy Getaways", a fictional travel website that allows users to book passage to some of the planets depicted in the film, including [Xandar](/wiki/Xandar "Xandar"), Morag, and Knowhere.[[196]](#cite_note-GalaxyGetaways-197) Beginning July 4, 2014, a sneak peek of the film was presented at [Disneyland](/wiki/Disneyland "Disneyland") and [Disney's Hollywood Studios](/wiki/Disney%27s_Hollywood_Studios "Disney's Hollywood Studios") in the [Magic Eye](/wiki/Captain_EO "Captain EO") and [ABC Sound Studio](/wiki/Sounds_Dangerous! "Sounds Dangerous!") theaters, respectively.[[197]](#cite_note-DisneyParksSneakPeek-198) Approximately 14 minutes of the film was screened on July 7, 2014, in IMAX 3D in the United States, and 3D theaters and IMAX 3D in Canada, along with two trailers.[[198]](#cite_note-IMAXScreening-199)[[199]](#cite_note-RamaScreening-200) The screening was met with positive reviews, praising the humor, the 3D, and IMAX conversion, and Cooper's portrayal of Rocket.[[199]](#cite_note-RamaScreening-200)[[200]](#cite_note-ColliderEarlyReaction-201)[[201]](#cite_note-CinemaBlendScreening-202)[[202]](#cite_note-CBRScreening-203) However, it was criticized for beginning partway through the film, not allowing viewers to easily acclimate to the film's tone,[[200]](#cite_note-ColliderEarlyReaction-201)[[203]](#cite_note-/FilmScreening-204) and for how the general audience might respond to a film within the MCU without established characters making appearances.[[203]](#cite_note-/FilmScreening-204)

On July 12, 2014, Gunn and the actors from the film promoted *Guardians of the Galaxy* at the [Lido 8 Cineplex](/wiki/Shaw_House_and_Centre "Shaw House and Centre") in Singapore.[[204]](#cite_note-SingaporePremiere-205) On July 17, 2014, [Disney Interactive](/wiki/Disney_Interactive "Disney Interactive") released an action [RPG](/wiki/Role_playing_game "Role playing game") [video game](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") titled *Guardians of the Galaxy: The Universal Weapon* for [iOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS"), [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)") and [Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows") devices.[[205]](#cite_note-MobileGame-206) The game's original story was written by [Dan Abnett](/wiki/Dan_Abnett "Dan Abnett"), and was meant to complement the film.[[206]](#cite_note-MobileGame2-207) On July 21, Pratt, Saldaña, Bautista, Diesel, and Cooper appeared on *Jimmy Kimmel Live!* to promote the film and debut some additional exclusive content.[[207]](#cite_note-KimmelPremiere-208) On July 29, Pratt and Saldaña rang the opening bell of the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") as part of the film's marketing strategy.[[208]](#cite_note-NYSE-209)

On August 14, Marvel released the scene of Groot dancing,[[209]](#cite_note-BabyGroot-210) which was shortly followed by an announcement from Funko that they were releasing a toy "Dancing Groot". *The Hollywood Reporter* noted that the quick release of the scene from Marvel two weeks after the film's theatrical release, along with the rush announcement from Funko, indicated the popularity of both the character and the scene.[[210]](#cite_note-THRBabyGroot-211) The scene also produced the word "grooting", coined by Michael Rooker, in which a person dances similar to Groot, with the word entering the social media [lexicon](/wiki/Lexicon "Lexicon").[[211]](#cite_note-Grooting-212) Also in August, Marvel held a special screening of the film at [Children's Hospital Los Angeles](/wiki/Children%27s_Hospital_Los_Angeles "Children's Hospital Los Angeles"), where Pratt dressed as Star-Lord to entertain the patients.[[212]](#cite_note-ChildrensHospitalScreening-213)

### Merchandise

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Merchandise")]

In June 2012, Marvel filed eleven [trademark](/wiki/Trademark "Trademark") applications for *Guardians of the Galaxy*, covering a variety of [consumer products](/wiki/Consumer_product "Consumer product"), from [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") to [cosmetics](/wiki/Cosmetics "Cosmetics").[[213]](#cite_note-2012TMApps-214) [Disney Consumer Products](/wiki/Disney_Consumer_Products "Disney Consumer Products") partnered with Mad Engine, C-Life, [New Era](/wiki/New_Era_Cap_Company "New Era Cap Company"), [Hasbro](/wiki/Hasbro "Hasbro"), Disguise, Rubies, [Sideshow Collectibles](/wiki/Sideshow_Collectibles "Sideshow Collectibles"), [Lego](/wiki/Lego "Lego"), KIDdesigns, [iHome](/wiki/IHome "IHome"), [Funko](/wiki/Funko "Funko"), Freeze, Fast Forward, and Innovative Designs to produce merchandise for the film, with releases starting in June 2014.[[195]](#cite_note-VarietyMarketingJun2014-196)[[214]](#cite_note-2013AugVariety-215)[[215]](#cite_note-LegoSets-216)[[216]](#cite_note-LegoSets2-217) Mad Engine and C-Life were partnered as the core apparel outfitters, producing a line of T-shirts and fleece jackets, while New Era produced headwear and hats.[[214]](#cite_note-2013AugVariety-215) Hasbro produced toys for the film; Disguise and Rubies produced the costumes; and [Sideshow Collectibles](/wiki/Sideshow_Collectibles "Sideshow Collectibles") was given charge of the collectibles.[[195]](#cite_note-VarietyMarketingJun2014-196)[[214]](#cite_note-2013AugVariety-215) Lego announced three toy sets based on scenes from the film,[[215]](#cite_note-LegoSets-216) while iHome created character speakers, Funko made vinyl [bobbleheads](/wiki/Bobblehead "Bobblehead"), Freeze crafted 1980s-inspired apparel, and backpacks and stationery were made by Fast Forward and Innovative Designs.[[195]](#cite_note-VarietyMarketingJun2014-196) Despite first films in a potential new franchise usually being off-limits to licensees, Marvel used *[Iron Man](/wiki/Iron_Man_(2008_film) "Iron Man (2008 film)")*'s success as evidence of unknown characters becoming hits with audiences to attract partnerships. Licensees embraced Rocket as the film's potential breakout character, with Drax and Gamora being used for older demographics; Star-Lord's obsession with 1980s nostalgia, including his "Awesome Mix Vo. 1" cassette, has also served as a basis for tie-in products.[[195]](#cite_note-VarietyMarketingJun2014-196) In August 2014, Funko announced a toy based on "Dancing Groot",[[210]](#cite_note-THRBabyGroot-211) while in October 2014, Marvel and KID designs announced a replica of dancing Groot, for release in December 2014.[[217]](#cite_note-KIDDancingGroot-218)

In December 2014, Disney made a second marketing push, to coincide with the film's home media release with additional merchandise. Merchandise partners included: KID designs with its replica dancing Groot; Funko's Fabrikations line with a plush Rocket; Mattel's Hot Wheels character cars; C-Life, Hybrid-Jem Sportswear, Freeze, MZ Berger, Accutime, AME and Her Universe with apparel; ThinkGeek with jewelry and watches; American Greetings with cards; Jay Franco with homegoods; and Vandor with drinkware. Additional partners included Mad Engine, Just Play, and Dragon Models. Paul Gitter, senior vice-president of Marvel licensing at Disney Consumer Products said, "The demand for *Guardians of the Galaxy* merchandise has been truly out of this world. Our merchandising and retail partners are doing a tremendous job of providing creative and innovative ways for fans to continue interacting with these popular characters and showcase their fandom year round."[[218]](#cite_note-Dec2014Marketing-219)

## Release

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Release")]

### Theatrical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Theatrical")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Karen_Gillan_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_London_premiere.jpg/250px-Karen_Gillan_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_London_premiere.jpg)](/wiki/File:Karen_Gillan_-_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_London_premiere.jpg)

Karen Gillan at the London premiere of *Guardians of the Galaxy*

The world premiere of *Guardians of the Galaxy* was held on July 21, 2014, at the [Dolby Theatre](/wiki/Dolby_Theatre "Dolby Theatre") in [Hollywood, Los Angeles](/wiki/Hollywood,_Los_Angeles "Hollywood, Los Angeles").[[207]](#cite_note-KimmelPremiere-208)[[219]](#cite_note-WorldPremiere-220) The film was released theatrically in the United Kingdom on July 31, 2014,[[161]](#cite_note-IGNMay2014-162) and in the United States on August 1,[[97]](#cite_note-2012SDCCPanel-98) in 3D and IMAX 3D.[[126]](#cite_note-2013Jan3D-127)[[220]](#cite_note-IMAX3D-221) The film was released in 4,080 theaters in the United States, making it the widest August release, breaking the five-year record held by *[G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra](/wiki/G.I._Joe:_The_Rise_of_Cobra "G.I. Joe: The Rise of Cobra")* (4,007 theaters). The breakdown of venues was: 354 [IMAX](/wiki/IMAX "IMAX") screens, 3,200 3D screens, 350 [large format](/wiki/Large_format_(photography) "Large format (photography)") screens, and 240 [D-Box](/wiki/D-Box_Technologies "D-Box Technologies") screens.[[221]](#cite_note-DeadlineReleaseFormats-222) In its sixth weekend, *Guardians of the Galaxy* was playing in 69 territories, its most.[[222]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek6-223) The film is part of [Phase Two](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Two "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Two") of the MCU.[[223]](#cite_note-PhaseTwo-224)

In June 2014, Gunn stated that the film had always been planned as a 3D film, and "Unlike many directors, I've been actively involved with converting every shot to 3D, making sure it works perfectly for the story and the film, making sure it's spectacular and immersive without being silly, distracting, or overly showy." Gunn also revealed that the IMAX 3D version would include shifting aspect ratios, to make the viewing experience "even fuller and more encompassing. I've personally chosen all the places where the changes occur ... The changing aspect ratios in this case are actually a part of the storytelling."[[224]](#cite_note-Gunn3D-225) In July 2014, Gunn revealed that there were multiple scenes he had cut from the film, and he was investigating how to release them, either in an extended cut of the film, or as bonus features on the film's home media release.[[69]](#cite_note-BrolinPerformance-70)

### Home media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Home media")]

*Guardians of the Galaxy* was released for [digital download](/wiki/Digital_distribution "Digital distribution") by [Walt Disney Studios Home Entertainment](/wiki/Walt_Disney_Studios_Home_Entertainment "Walt Disney Studios Home Entertainment") on November 18, 2014, and on [Blu-ray](/wiki/Blu-ray "Blu-ray"), [Blu-ray 3D](/wiki/Blu-ray_Disc#Blu-ray_3D "Blu-ray Disc"), and [DVD](/wiki/DVD-Video "DVD-Video") on November 24, 2014, in the United Kingdom and on December 9 in the United States.[[225]](#cite_note-UKBlu-ray-226)[[226]](#cite_note-Blu-ray-227) The digital and Blu-ray releases include behind-the-scenes featurettes, audio commentary, deleted scenes, a blooper reel, and an exclusive preview of *[Avengers: Age of Ultron](/wiki/Avengers:_Age_of_Ultron "Avengers: Age of Ultron")*.[[226]](#cite_note-Blu-ray-227) As of October 4, 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit), the film has earned over $118 million in sales in the US.[[227]](#cite_note-HomeSales-228)

The film was also included in the 13-disc [box set](/wiki/Box_set "Box set"), titled "[Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Two Collection](/wiki/Marvel_Cinematic_Universe:_Phase_Two#Home_media "Marvel Cinematic Universe: Phase Two")", which includes all of the Phase Two films in the Marvel Cinematic Universe. It was released on December 8, 2015.[[228]](#cite_note-Boxset-229) The IMAX Enhanced version of the film was made available on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") beginning on November 12, 2021.[[229]](#cite_note-IMAXEnhanced-230)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Reception")]

> "We wanted to do a space movie, and we loved the [Guardians comics](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy "Guardians of the Galaxy"). Just the ridiculous pairing of a [tree](/wiki/Groot "Groot") and a [raccoon](/wiki/Rocket_Raccoon "Rocket Raccoon"). It was before *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars")* came back with a vengeance. It was like, "Let's try a big space movie, the kind of which hasn't been around for a while." The audience is following us to these places. The success of *[Ant-Man](/wiki/Ant-Man_(film) "Ant-Man (film)")* and *[Doctor Strange](/wiki/Doctor_Strange_(2016_film) "Doctor Strange (2016 film)")* helped us go, "Hey, the audience is with us," and *Guardians* is probably the best example of how far they [the audience] are willing to go."

—[Kevin Feige](/wiki/Kevin_Feige "Kevin Feige"), President of Marvel Studios, on the success of the first *Guardians of the Galaxy* film[[230]](#cite_note-FeigeInterviewVanityFair-231)

### Box office

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Box office")]

*Guardians of the Galaxy* earned $333.7 million in North America and an estimated $439.6 million in other countries, for a worldwide total of $773.3 million.[[3]](#cite_note-BOM-3) The film became the third-highest-grossing film in the Marvel Cinematic Universe, behind *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(2012_film) "The Avengers (2012 film)")* and *[Iron Man 3](/wiki/Iron_Man_3 "Iron Man 3")*.[[231]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek12-232) It was the third-highest-grossing [2014 film](/wiki/2014_in_film "2014 in film") (behind *[Transformers: Age of Extinction](/wiki/Transformers:_Age_of_Extinction "Transformers: Age of Extinction")* and *[The Hobbit: The Battle of the Five Armies](/wiki/The_Hobbit:_The_Battle_of_the_Five_Armies "The Hobbit: The Battle of the Five Armies")*) and the highest-grossing superhero film of 2014.[[2]](#cite_note-ActualBudget-2)[[232]](#cite_note-BOMWorldwide-233)[[233]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek13-234) It had a worldwide opening weekend of $160.7 million.[[234]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek1-235) *[Deadline Hollywood](/wiki/Deadline_Hollywood "Deadline Hollywood")* calculated the film's net profit as $204.2 million, accounting for production budgets, marketing, talent participations, and other costs; box office grosses and home media revenues placed it fifth on their list of 2014's "Most Valuable Blockbusters".[[235]](#cite_note-DeadlineNetProfit-236)

#### North America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: North America")]

*Guardians of the Galaxy* earned $11.2 million on its Thursday night pre-opening, surpassing *Captain America: The Winter Soldier*'s gross ($10.2 million) for the biggest Thursday evening start for a movie in 2014.[[236]](#cite_note-VarietyThursdayEarningPlusIntl-237) IMAX accounted for 17% of the total gross ($1.9 million), which was the biggest August pre-release in IMAX format.[[221]](#cite_note-DeadlineReleaseFormats-222) On its opening day, the film earned $37.8 million, including the Thursday night earnings.[[237]](#cite_note-OpeningDayTotal-238) *Guardians of the Galaxy* was the number one movie during its opening weekend and grossed $94.3 million, setting an August weekend record, surpassing *[The Bourne Ultimatum](/wiki/The_Bourne_Ultimatum_(film) "The Bourne Ultimatum (film)")*.[[238]](#cite_note-OpeningWeekendBoxOffice-239) During the opening weekend, IMAX earnings amounted to $11.7 million and 3-D showings accounted for 45% of ticket sales. The film's success was partially attributed to its appeal to both genders: the opening-weekend audience was 44% female, which is the biggest proportion ever for a MCU film; 55% of the opening-weekend audience was over the age of 25.[[238]](#cite_note-OpeningWeekendBoxOffice-239)

Although the film fell to second place in its second and third weekends, behind *[Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles](/wiki/Teenage_Mutant_Ninja_Turtles_(2014_film) "Teenage Mutant Ninja Turtles (2014 film)")*,[[239]](#cite_note-Forbes2ndWknd-240)[[240]](#cite_note-LATimes3rdWknd-241) the film was number one in its fourth, fifth, and sixth weekends.[[241]](#cite_note-Variety4thWknd-242)[[242]](#cite_note-Variety5thWkndFinal-243)[[243]](#cite_note-Variety6thWknd-244) By doing so, it became the first film in 2014 to top the domestic box office in non-consecutive weeks,[[241]](#cite_note-Variety4thWknd-242) the first film of the summer (May–August) to be the number one film in three weekends[[244]](#cite_note-ThreeWkndNum1-245) and the first MCU film to be the top film for four weeks, surpassing *Captain America: The Winter Soldier* and *The Avengers*, both of which were number one for three weeks, and tied *[The Dark Knight](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight "The Dark Knight")* for the most weeks at number one among comic book-based films.[[245]](#cite_note-MostMarvelNo1-246) Phil Contrino, vice president and chief analyst of *[BoxOffice.com](/wiki/Boxoffice_(magazine) "Boxoffice (magazine)")* felt *Guardians* success was "unconventional" and was "shattering expectations".[[242]](#cite_note-Variety5thWkndFinal-243) The film remained in the top 10 for ten weekends.[[246]](#cite_note-Deadline10thWknd-247)

The film was the top-grossing film of summer 2014,[[247]](#cite_note-Mojo4thWknd-248)[[248]](#cite_note-DeadlineTopSummer-249) first of 2014 to pass $300 million for its domestic gross,[[249]](#cite_note-Variety300milDomestic-250) and was the third-highest-grossing domestic film of 2014 (behind *[American Sniper](/wiki/American_Sniper "American Sniper")* and *[The Hunger Games: Mockingjay – Part 1](/wiki/The_Hunger_Games:_Mockingjay_%E2%80%93_Part_1 "The Hunger Games: Mockingjay – Part 1")*).[[250]](#cite_note-BOM-Highest-2014-251) The film was said to have "injected life" into an otherwise lower-than-normal summer box office.[[242]](#cite_note-Variety5thWkndFinal-243)[[248]](#cite_note-DeadlineTopSummer-249)[[251]](#cite_note-InjectLife-252)

#### Other territories

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Other territories")]

*Guardians of the Galaxy* was released in 42 international markets and grossed $66.4 million on its opening weekend. The biggest debuts came from [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") ($13 million), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") ($10.8 million), [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") ($6.5 million), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil") ($6.5 million), and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") ($4.7 million).[[234]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek1-235) The film topped the weekend box office two times, in its first and second weekends.[[234]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek1-235)[[252]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek2-253) In its eleventh weekend, the film opened in China, earning $29.8 million, its largest, and became the third-highest opening in the country for any Disney release, behind *Iron Man 3* and *Captain America: The Winter Soldier*, and was an all-time industry record opening in October.[[253]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek11-254) The following weekend saw an additional $21.3 million from China, making China the highest-grossing market with $69 million.[[231]](#cite_note-DeadlineIntWeek12-232) The film's three biggest markets in total earnings were: China ($96.5 million), the UK ($47.4 million), and Russia ($37.5 million).[[3]](#cite_note-BOM-3)

### Critical response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Critical response")]

The [review aggregator](/wiki/Review_aggregator "Review aggregator") [Rotten Tomatoes](/wiki/Rotten_Tomatoes "Rotten Tomatoes") reported an approval rating of 91%, with an average score of 7.8/10, based on 337 reviews. The website's consensus reads, "*Guardians of the Galaxy* is just as irreverent as fans of the frequently zany Marvel comic would expect—as well as funny, thrilling, full of heart, and packed with visual splendor."[[254]](#cite_note-255) [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic") gave the film a [weighted average](/wiki/Weighted_arithmetic_mean "Weighted arithmetic mean") score of 76 out of 100, based on 53 critics, indicating "generally favorable" reviews.[[255]](#cite_note-256) Audiences polled by [CinemaScore](/wiki/CinemaScore "CinemaScore") gave the film an average grade of "A" on an A+ to F scale,[[256]](#cite_note-CinemaScore-257) while earning an "A+" among under-18 and 25- to 34-year-old viewers.[[257]](#cite_note-ForbesMendelson-258)

Scott Foundas of *[Variety](/wiki/Variety_(magazine) "Variety (magazine)")* said "James Gunn's presumptive franchise-starter is overlong, overstuffed, and sometimes too eager to please, but the cheeky comic tone keeps things buoyant—as does Chris Pratt's winning performance", and praised the film's look created by cinematographer [Ben Davis](/wiki/Ben_Davis_(cinematographer) "Ben Davis (cinematographer)"), production designer Charles Wood, and special effects makeup designer David White.[[172]](#cite_note-VarietyReview-173) Justin Lowe of *[The Hollywood Reporter](/wiki/The_Hollywood_Reporter "The Hollywood Reporter")* also praised the film's look, and felt "A well-matched ensemble rises to the challenge of launching a heroic origin film with distinctive style, abundant thrills, and no shortage of humor."[[258]](#cite_note-THRReview-259) *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")*'s Robbie Collin said, "A brand new summer family blockbuster this may be, but it plays by old, half-forgotten rules; trimming out the clutter and cross-referencing for snappy, streamlined, Saturday-cartoon fun".[[259]](#cite_note-TelegraphReview-260) [Kenneth Turan](/wiki/Kenneth_Turan "Kenneth Turan") of the *[Los Angeles Times](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Times "Los Angeles Times")* said, "Blessed with a loose, anarchic B-picture soul that encourages you to enjoy yourself even when you're not quite sure what's going on, the scruffy *Guardians* is irreverent in a way that can bring the first *[Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars_(film) "Star Wars (film)")* to mind, in part because it has some of the most unconventional heroes this side of the [Mos Eisley](/wiki/Mos_Eisley "Mos Eisley") cantina."[[260]](#cite_note-LATimesReview-261) [Manohla Dargis](/wiki/Manohla_Dargis "Manohla Dargis") of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* said, "While *Guardians* takes you down one after another crazy narrative turn, it also pulls you into—and, for the most part, keeps you in—a fully realized other world."[[261]](#cite_note-NYTimesReview-262) [Richard Roeper](/wiki/Richard_Roeper "Richard Roeper") of the *[Chicago Sun-Times](/wiki/Chicago_Sun-Times "Chicago Sun-Times")* said, "*Guardians of the Galaxy* is a late summer treat—a mostly lighthearted and self-referential comic-book movie with loads of whiz-bang action, some laugh-out-loud moments, and a couple of surprisingly beautiful and touching scenes as well," calling it "a refreshing confection of entertainment".[[262]](#cite_note-ChicagoTimesReview-263)

Jake Coyle of the *[Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press")* was more critical of the film, calling it "terribly overstuffed and many of the jokes get drowned out by the special effects ... The pervasive movie references detract from the stab at freshness, and *Guardians* depends all too much on the whimsy of '70s anthems for an original beat." He also felt that Close, Reilly, and del Toro were underused in the film.[[263]](#cite_note-APReview-264) [Mick LaSalle](/wiki/Mick_LaSalle "Mick LaSalle") of the *[San Francisco Chronicle](/wiki/San_Francisco_Chronicle "San Francisco Chronicle")* said, "In place of wit, *Guardians* offers a sort of generalized willingness to be amusing, an atmosphere of high spirits that feels like lots of people pumping air into a tire that has a hole in it. Everyone is clearly working, but nothing is really happening—and yet the effort is so evident that there's an impulse to reward it."[[264]](#cite_note-SFChronicleReview-265) The film received mixed reviews in China, according to [state media](/wiki/State_media "State media") outlet *[China Daily](/wiki/China_Daily "China Daily")*, where viewers complained that the film's "poor subtitle translation not only spoiled the fun of watching it, but also made it difficult to understand its humor".[[265]](#cite_note-ChinaDaily-266) [Jim Starlin](/wiki/Jim_Starlin "Jim Starlin"), creator of Drax, Gamora, and Thanos, said it "might be Marvel's best movie yet".[[266]](#cite_note-Starlin-267) Director [Steven Spielberg](/wiki/Steven_Spielberg "Steven Spielberg") said that the film was the superhero film "that impressed [him] most" as it does "not take itself too seriously". He felt he had "seen something new in [superhero] movies [from the film], without any cynicism, or fear of being dark when needed".[[267]](#cite_note-StevenSpielberg-268)

In 2017, *Guardians of the Galaxy* was featured as one of the 100 greatest films of all time in *[Empire](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine) "Empire (film magazine)")* magazine's poll of [The 100 Greatest Movies](/wiki/Empire_(film_magazine)#The_100_Greatest_Movies "Empire (film magazine)"), as the highest MCU film on the list.[[268]](#cite_note-Empire100Movies-269)

### Accolades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Accolades")]

Main article: [List of accolades received by *Guardians of the Galaxy* (film)](/wiki/List_of_accolades_received_by_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film) "List of accolades received by Guardians of the Galaxy (film)")

At the [87th Academy Awards](/wiki/87th_Academy_Awards "87th Academy Awards"), *Guardians of the Galaxy* received nominations for [Best Makeup and Hairstyling](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Makeup_and_Hairstyling "Academy Award for Best Makeup and Hairstyling") and [Best Visual Effects](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Visual_Effects "Academy Award for Best Visual Effects").[[269]](#cite_note-AcademyAwards-270) The film's other nominations include an [Annie Award](/wiki/Annie_Award "Annie Award"),[[270]](#cite_note-AnnieAwards-271) two [British Academy Film Awards](/wiki/British_Academy_Film_Awards "British Academy Film Awards"),[[271]](#cite_note-BAFTA-272) and five [Critics' Choice Movie Awards](/wiki/Critics%27_Choice_Movie_Awards "Critics' Choice Movie Awards") (winning two).[[272]](#cite_note-CriticsChoiceAwards-273)

## Sequels and special

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Sequels and special")]

Further information: [List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films](/wiki/List_of_Marvel_Cinematic_Universe_films "List of Marvel Cinematic Universe films")

### *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2")]

Main article: [*Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2*](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._2 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2")

*Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 2* was released on May 5, 2017, again written and directed by James Gunn.[[273]](#cite_note-VarietyVol2-274) Pratt, Saldaña, Bautista, Diesel, Cooper, Rooker, Gillan, and Sean Gunn all reprise their roles in the film,[[274]](#cite_note-GotG2Cast-275) and are joined by [Pom Klementieff](/wiki/Pom_Klementieff "Pom Klementieff") as [Mantis](/wiki/Mantis_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Mantis (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"),[[5]](#cite_note-Vol1Title2-5)[[275]](#cite_note-KlementieffGotG2-276) [Elizabeth Debicki](/wiki/Elizabeth_Debicki "Elizabeth Debicki") as [Ayesha](/wiki/Ayesha_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ayesha (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"), [Chris Sullivan](/wiki/Chris_Sullivan_(actor) "Chris Sullivan (actor)") as [Taserface](/wiki/Taserface "Taserface"), and [Kurt Russell](/wiki/Kurt_Russell "Kurt Russell") as Quill's father [Ego](/wiki/Ego_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Ego (Marvel Cinematic Universe)").[[5]](#cite_note-Vol1Title2-5)[[274]](#cite_note-GotG2Cast-275)

### *The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special")]

Main article: [*The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special*](/wiki/The_Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Holiday_Special "The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special")

*The Guardians of the Galaxy Holiday Special* is a [Marvel Studios Special Presentation](/wiki/Marvel_Studios_Special_Presentation "Marvel Studios Special Presentation") that was released on [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+") on November 25, 2022,[[276]](#cite_note-HSMSSP-277)[[277]](#cite_note-HSRelease-278) with Gunn writing and directing.[[278]](#cite_note-2023Vol3Release-279) Pratt, Bautista, Diesel, Cooper, Gillan, Klementieff, Rooker, and Sean Gunn reprise their roles in the special,[[279]](#cite_note-HSCast-280) which is set before the events of *Vol. 3*.[[280]](#cite_note-HSTimeline-281)

### *Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_(film)&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3")]

Main article: [*Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3*](/wiki/Guardians_of_the_Galaxy_Vol._3 "Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3")

*Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3* was released on May 5, 2023,[[281]](#cite_note-Vol3ReleaseDate-282) with Gunn returning to write and direct.[[282]](#cite_note-GunnVol3Confirm-283)[[283]](#cite_note-GunnRehired-284) Pratt, Saldaña, Bautista, Diesel, Cooper,[[284]](#cite_note-Vol3MainCast-285) Gillan,[[285]](#cite_note-GillanVol3-286) Klementieff,[[286]](#cite_note-KlementieffVol3-287) and Sean Gunn reprise their roles in the film,[[45]](#cite_note-SeanGunnSept2018-46) and are joined by [Will Poulter](/wiki/Will_Poulter "Will Poulter") as [Adam Warlock](/wiki/Adam_Warlock_(Marvel_Cinematic_Universe) "Adam Warlock (Marvel Cinematic Universe)"),[[287]](#cite_note-Poulter-288) [Maria Bakalova](/wiki/Maria_Bakalova "Maria Bakalova") as [Cosmo the Spacedog](/wiki/Cosmo_the_Spacedog "Cosmo the Spacedog"), and [Chukwudi Iwuji](/wiki/Chukwudi_Iwuji "Chukwudi Iwuji") as Rocket's creator, the High Evolutionary.
